planting.note: Set divisions out as new growth begins in early spring or plant in early fall.

directions: Plant the rhizome just below the soil surface in humus-rich, well-drained ground under high shade. Shear the old foliage back in late winter so the coppery-orange spring flowers stand clear above fresh leaves. Water through the first summer while roots establish, then it tolerates dry shade once settled.

planting.note: Divide and plant in spring or early autumn while the soil is warm and moist.

directions: Give this Chinese species a sheltered, shaded spot with leafy, moisture-retentive soil. The white-and-deep-purple pendant flowers open in mid-spring, so trim tired leaves in late winter to show them off. Keep the crown mulched with leaf mold and it slowly forms a tidy evergreen clump.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into cool, shaded, humus-rich soil.

directions: Set the shallow rhizome just under a loose layer of leaf mold in dappled or full shade. Large pure-white spurred flowers appear in spring above the low mound of foliage. Protect it from drying wind and keep it evenly moist, and it will spread gently into a slow colony.

planting.note: Lift and divide clumps in early spring, replanting immediately in shade.

directions: Plant in fertile woodland soil that stays cool through summer, siting it out of hot afternoon sun. Large nodding white flowers with long spurs hang above the leaves in spring, so cut old foliage away before bloom. A yearly topdressing of compost keeps the clump vigorous.

planting.note: Divide in spring and grow on in a shaded, sheltered bed.

directions: This long-flowering Chinese species wants loose, humus-rich soil in light shade with steady moisture. Its spidery yellow flowers with long curved spurs open over many weeks in spring and early summer. Mulch the shallow rhizomes each autumn and shelter the semi-evergreen leaves from harsh winter wind.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a cool, shaded position.

directions: Grow this compact species in leafy, well-drained soil in dappled or deep shade. Small clear-yellow flowers appear in spring above neat bronze-tinted new leaves. Keep it evenly moist and top-dress with leaf mold, and it forms a low, spreading carpet over time.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring into shaded, humus-rich soil.

directions: Site in cool woodland conditions with reliable moisture and shelter from midday sun. Dainty bell-shaped yellow-and-maroon flowers dangle above the foliage in mid-spring. Cut back winter-tattered leaves before the flush of new growth to display the blooms.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in sheltered, evenly moist shade.

directions: This evergreen Chinese species carries airy sprays of many tiny white flowers over marbled new foliage in late spring. Give it rich, moisture-retentive soil in a shaded, wind-sheltered spot. Leave the handsome mottled leaves through winter in mild areas and only tidy damaged growth in early spring.

planting.note: Divide in spring and grow on in cool woodland shade.

directions: Plant the shallow rhizome in loose, leaf-rich soil in dappled to full shade. Slender pale-lilac and white flowers with prominent stamens open in spring above bronze-flushed leaflets. Keep the root run cool and moist and mulch yearly with composted leaves.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring into sheltered, humus-rich shade.

directions: Named for its stiff, spiny-edged evergreen leaves, this species prefers rich, cool soil in light shade. Pale yellow spurred flowers rise on wiry stems in spring. Shelter the foliage from cold drying winds and refresh the mulch every autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn in cool shade.

directions: This dainty hybrid opens soft rose-pink flowers with paler spurs above mottled young foliage in spring. Grow it in fertile, well-drained woodland soil in dappled shade. Trim away old leaves in late winter and keep it lightly moist through the growing season.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring into rich, shaded soil.

directions: This selection of the Japanese longspur barrenwort bears large rosy-red flowers with long pale spurs in spring. Plant in humus-rich, moisture-retentive soil in part shade, sheltered from strong sun. Cut the old deciduous foliage to the ground in late winter so the flowers and coppery new leaves show cleanly.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in part shade.

directions: A tough, spreading hybrid grown for coppery-red and yellow spring flowers over bronze-tinted new leaves. Set the rhizomes just below the surface in any decent soil in dappled shade. Shear old foliage in late winter, and once established it copes well with dry shade under trees.

planting.note: Divide in spring and grow in cool, sheltered shade.

directions: This hybrid carries large spidery flowers of soft yellow and rose with elongated spurs in spring. Give it fertile, humus-rich soil in dappled shade with steady moisture. Remove tired old leaves before bloom, and mulch the crown with leaf mold each autumn.

planting.note: Plant dormant tubers in autumn or early spring about 4 inches deep.

directions: Set the tuber roughly four inches deep in humus-rich, sharply drained woodland soil in shade. In spring it produces a striking dark-purple-and-white striped spathe wrapped around a bright white club-shaped spadix. Keep it moist during growth, then let it dry as the leaves yellow and it goes dormant by late summer.

planting.note: Plant tubers 3 to 4 inches deep in autumn or spring.

directions: This diminutive species bears small hooded yellow spathes marked with dark purple in late spring and early summer. Plant the tuber several inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil in dappled shade. It self-sows freely where happy, so leave the ripening berry clusters if you want seedlings.

planting.note: Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in autumn or early spring.

directions: One of the tallest cobra lilies, it sends up a green spathe from which a long whip-like spadix curves upward and out. Plant the large tuber deeply in rich, moist but well-drained soil in light shade. It is easy and vigorous, dying back to dormancy after the leaves fade in late summer.

planting.note: Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in a sheltered shaded bed.

directions: This elegant species carries a whorl of narrow leaflets like an umbrella, each often ending in a fine drip-tip thread, topped by a striped green-and-white spathe. Plant the tuber deeply in fertile, moisture-retentive woodland soil in dappled shade. Emerging late in spring, it appreciates a summer mulch and shelter from strong wind.

planting.note: Plant tubers 4 to 5 inches deep in humus-rich shade.

directions: Grown for its extraordinary broad, netted-purple spathe with flaring hooded lips and a long tail-like spadix. Plant the tuber in cool, leafy, well-drained soil in shade sheltered from hot sun. Keep moist while in leaf and allow a dry rest once the foliage dies down.

planting.note: Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in sheltered woodland shade.

directions: A dramatic species with a large three-part leaf on a mottled stalk and a deep maroon-and-white striped spathe carrying a very long trailing spadix. Plant the tuber deeply in rich, moist, free-draining soil in a warm, shaded, sheltered spot. In colder gardens mulch heavily over winter or lift and store the tuber dry.

planting.note: Plant tubers 3 to 4 inches deep in autumn or early spring.

directions: One of the hardiest cobra lilies, this compact species from northeast Asia bears a green-and-white striped spathe in spring above a single divided leaf. Plant the tuber a few inches deep in cool, humus-rich woodland soil in part shade. Reliable in cold climates, it dies back to dormancy after the summer berries color to red.

planting.note: Plant tubers 4 to 5 inches deep in rich, shaded soil.

directions: This species is known for a horizontal ring of many leaflets and a green spathe from which a long, curving spadix extends far out like a crane's neck. Plant the tuber in fertile, moisture-retentive soil in dappled shade. Vigorous and easy, it multiplies readily and appreciates a yearly feed of leaf mold.

planting.note: Plant tubers 4 to 5 inches deep in humus-rich shade.

directions: This Japanese cobra lily produces a dark, near-black spathe from which an exceptionally long thread-like spadix rises and trails downward like a fishing line. Plant the tuber in cool, leafy, well-drained soil in shade. Keep it moist during growth and give it a dry dormant rest once the leaf withers.

planting.note: Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in a warm, sheltered shade bed.

directions: Among the earliest to emerge, it pushes up a stout mottled stalk topped by a broad brown-and-cream spathe resembling a pitcher plant. Plant the tuber deeply in rich, free-draining woodland soil in a warm, sheltered shaded spot. Guard the early growth against late frosts and mulch well over winter.

planting.note: Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in rich, shaded soil.

directions: This bold species has a large three-lobed leaf and a broad, glossy purple-and-white striped spathe with rounded flaring lips. Plant the tuber deeply in fertile, moisture-retentive but well-drained soil in dappled shade. It emerges in late spring, so a light marker helps you avoid disturbing the resting tuber.

planting.note: Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in humus-rich shade.

directions: A large, striking species with a single big three-part leaf on a mottled stem and a maroon-and-white striped spathe drawn out into a long trailing tail. Plant the tuber deeply in rich, moisture-retentive woodland soil in sheltered shade. Keep it moist through summer growth and mulch the crown well against winter cold.

planting.note: Divide creeping rhizomes in spring and plant in shaded, humus-rich soil.

directions: This spreading Japanese perennial has arching stems lined with white-striped leaves and pale tubular flowers dangling from the tips in spring. Plant the shallow rhizomes in cool, moist, leafy soil in shade and it slowly colonizes to form a groundcover. Keep it evenly watered in summer and shelter the variegated foliage from scorching sun.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring and plant in sheltered, rich shade.

directions: Grown for its tall, bamboo-like stems and dark purple-bronze new growth that matures to green, tipped with small greenish bell flowers in late spring. Plant divisions in fertile, moisture-retentive soil in dappled shade sheltered from wind. Cut old canes to the base in late winter and mulch to protect the crown in colder gardens.

planting.note: Divide in spring and grow in rich, sheltered shade.

directions: This graceful Chinese species forms clumps of upright, branching stems hung with narrow greenish-yellow bells in spring, followed by black berries. Plant divisions in cool, humus-rich, moisture-retentive soil in part shade. Shelter it from cold winds and keep the roots mulched and evenly moist through summer.

planting.note: Divide rhizomes in spring and plant in shaded woodland soil.

directions: This hardy species from northeast Asia carries pale greenish-white bells at the tips of upright stems in late spring, followed by blue-black berries. Plant divisions in cool, leafy, moisture-retentive soil in part to full shade. Keep evenly moist through the growing season and mulch the crown each autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in cool, sharply drained, humus-rich shade.

directions: This choice Japanese woodlander produces a whorl of broad leaves crowned by a single starry white flower in late spring. Plant in cool, gritty, humus-rich soil that never dries out or waterlogs, in dappled or full shade. It is slow and long-lived, so give it a permanent, undisturbed spot and mulch each autumn with leaf mold.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, shaded woodland soil.

directions: This curious woodlander bears a single whorl of four broad leaves topped by a green spidery flower that ripens to a glossy black berry. Plant the creeping rhizome shallowly in cool, moist, humus-rich soil in shade. All parts are poisonous, so site it away from where children forage, and leave it undisturbed to slowly spread.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, sheltered, humus-rich shade.

directions: This refined Japanese woodlander sends up airy stems of nodding waxy flowers in soft lilac and cream in late summer, when little else blooms in shade. Plant in cool, deep, moisture-retentive soil rich in leaf mold, in a sheltered spot out of drying wind and hot sun. It resents disturbance and drought, so keep it evenly moist and leave the clump to mature undisturbed.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in rich, sheltered shade.

directions: This bold species unfurls large glossy, deeply lobed umbrella leaves above which clusters of deep maroon-red flowers hang in spring. Plant the fleshy rhizome just below the surface in deep, humus-rich, moisture-retentive soil in sheltered shade. Keep it well watered while in leaf and mulch generously, as the emerging shoots can be caught by late frost.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in fertile, moist, shaded soil.

directions: This popular hybrid is prized for large umbrella leaves boldly mottled in chocolate-brown over green, with clusters of dark red flowers dangling beneath in spring. Plant the rhizome shallowly in rich, moisture-retentive woodland soil in shade sheltered from wind. Keep it consistently moist through summer and top-dress with leaf mold each autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in cool, constantly moist shade.

directions: Named for white petals that turn translucent like glass when wet with rain, this Asian umbrella-leaf carries clusters of small white flowers above broad lobed leaves in late spring. Plant in deep, humus-rich soil that stays reliably moist, in cool full or dappled shade. It needs steady moisture and cool roots, so mulch heavily and never let it dry out.

planting.note: Autumn planting suits this clump-forming ornamental onion best.

directions: Set the bulb or division 3 to 4 inches deep in average, well-drained soil. This late-season selection makes a tidy mound of glaucous blue foliage crowned by rounded rosy-purple flower balls from late summer into fall. Pollinators love it; simply shear back the tired foliage after frost.

planting.note: Plant the large bulb in autumn to root before winter.

directions: Plant bulbs 6 to 7 inches deep in a sunny, sharply drained spot. Tall stems lift large ivory-white globes flecked with a green eye in late spring, superb for cutting and drying. Water while in active growth, then keep the ground on the dry side once leaves brown.

planting.note: Plant bulbs or divisions in autumn for late-summer flowering the following year.

directions: Plant 2 to 3 inches deep in lean, well-drained soil in full sun. The signature swirling, blue-gray leaves form a low pinwheel rosette, topped by domed pink flower clusters in late summer. Very drought tolerant once settled; divide clumps every few years to reinvigorate the twist.

planting.note: Set out in fall; this is one of the last ornamental onions to bloom each season.

directions: Plant divisions or bulbs about 3 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil. Grassy foliage stays neat all season before rosy-purple to magenta pompoms open in autumn, often into October. A valuable late nectar source; leave the seed heads for winter interest.

planting.note: Plant the small bulbs in autumn for a novelty spring-into-summer display.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in a sunny spot with sharp drainage. Each head is a whimsical burst of green, hair-like tendrils tangled with tiny purple bulbils, appearing late spring into early summer. It is a curiosity for arrangements; deadhead if you want to limit its self-sowing.

planting.note: Plant in autumn several weeks before the ground freezes.

directions: Plant bulbs 6 to 8 inches deep in gritty, free-draining soil and full sun. Among the earliest of the tall drumstick types, it opens loose spheres of star-shaped purple-violet florets with protruding stamens in mid to late spring. Foliage fades early, so pair it with later perennials to hide the gap.

planting.note: Plant bulbs on their side in autumn to keep water out of the hollow crown.

directions: Set the bulb 6 to 8 inches deep, angled slightly, in deep, sharply drained soil. This form flaunts a crown of nodding brick-red to orange-red bells beneath a leafy topknot in mid spring. Provide full sun, avoid summer wet, and mulch lightly in colder zones.

planting.note: Plant bulbs on their side in autumn in free-draining soil.

directions: Plant the bulb 6 to 8 inches deep, tipped to one side to prevent rot. It produces a ring of clear golden-yellow pendant bells under a green leafy tuft in mid spring. Give it full sun and gritty soil, and let it bake dry through summer once the foliage yellows.

planting.note: Plant the small bulbs in autumn in gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 inches deep in very well-drained, gritty soil, excellent for a rock garden or trough. Short stems bear clusters of small nodding bells, rich mahogany-maroon rimmed with bright golden-yellow, in mid spring. Keep it dry in summer dormancy and protect from winter wet.

planting.note: Plant the bulbs promptly in early autumn before they dry out.

directions: Plant the elongated bulbs 4 to 5 inches deep in cool, humus-rich, moist but well-drained soil, without letting them dry beforehand. This vigorous hybrid lifts several nodding sulphur-yellow flowers with reflexed petals above glossy, mottled leaves in mid spring. Ideal for a woodland garden; give it dappled shade and leave clumps to multiply.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn once soil has cooled but well before it freezes solid.

directions: Set the small bulbs about 3 inches deep and 3 inches apart in a well-drained spot that gets sun in early spring. Drifts of dozens naturalize quickly and self-sow, so give them room to spread under deciduous trees. Star-shaped blue flowers with white centers open just as the snow retreats.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn before hard frost.

directions: Set the bulbs 3 inches deep and 3 inches apart in fall in a bright, well-drained position. The blooms open a rich violet-purple washed toward white at the center, deeper than the ordinary blue forms. Combine with early crocus for a saturated early-spring effect.

planting.note: Set out in autumn among snowdrops and early crocus.

directions: Plant the bulbs 3 inches deep in well-drained soil in fall. This pure-white form drops the blue striping and reads as a crisp white spike in early spring. Grow it in loose drifts at the front of a border or scattered through a rock garden.

planting.note: Best moved 'in the green' after bloom, or plant fresh bulbs in early fall.

directions: Plant 3 inches deep in cool, moisture-retentive soil in light shade. This old double form packs a tight rosette of green-marked inner segments inside the white outer petals. It bulks up steadily by offsets and is easily increased by lifting and splitting clumps after flowering.

planting.note: Plant in fall, or move 'in the green' after flowering for reliable establishment.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 inches deep in fall in cool, fertile soil in light shade. This robust, award-winning cultivar bears large, rounded, honey-scented white flowers on strong stems. It bulks up well and is one of the most dependable garden snowdrops for beginners.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a sheltered, part-shaded spot for early bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs about 3 inches deep in fall in rich, well-drained soil in dappled shade. This early, vigorous old cultivar carries long, elegant white flowers on tall stems and multiplies quickly. Lift and divide crowded clumps after flowering to keep colonies vigorous.

planting.note: Plant in fall; the long flower stalks show best where they can sway freely.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 inches deep in fall in cool, humus-rich soil in light shade. This classic cultivar is famous for its exceptionally long, slender flower stalks that let the blooms dance in the slightest breeze. It is a strong grower that naturalizes into generous drifts over time.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a warm, sharply drained spot.

directions: Plant the small bulbs about 3 to 4 inches deep in fall in gritty, fast-draining soil in full sun. The fragrant violet-blue flowers with gold crests on the falls appear before the grassy leaves lengthen. Give the bulbs a warm, dry summer baking to encourage them to return and flower reliably.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in fall in a sunny, well-drained bed or rock garden. This popular selection opens sky-blue to royal-blue flowers with a bright yellow ridge and white markings on the falls. Combine it in pots or at the border front where its early color can be seen up close.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a sunny, sharply drained position.

directions: Plant the bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in fall in gritty soil that dries out in summer. This soft, pale Cambridge-blue selection carries an orange ridge on each fall and is delicately scented. Plant it in generous clusters, as the small flowers make the most impact in numbers.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in full sun with fast drainage.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in fall in a warm, well-drained spot. This selection is prized for its deep reddish-purple flowers with a golden crest and a light violet fragrance. Let the bulbs bake through summer dormancy to keep them flowering year after year.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a sunny, gritty, well-drained site.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in fall in sharply drained soil in full sun. This famous hybrid opens intricately marked flowers in pale ice-blue and primrose, netted and speckled with darker blue and gold. It is vigorous and one of the most reliable of the early dwarf irises for naturalizing.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a sunny, warm, free-draining position.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in fall in gritty soil in full sun. This early, robust selection bears large, rich reddish-purple flowers with a golden ridge, opening before the leaves develop. It flowers dependably in its first year and makes an excellent container subject for late winter.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in fall in gritty, sharply drained soil. This selection opens deep violet-purple flowers with a striking white blaze and gold ridge on the falls. Site it where a summer baking keeps the bulbs firm and flowering.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a sunny, freely draining bed.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in fall in gritty soil in full sun. This selection shows clear sky-blue flowers with a bold yellow blaze running down each fall. It is early and vigorous, ideal for the front of a rock garden or a winter container.

planting.note: Plant in fall in cool, gritty soil that stays a little moister than other reticulatas prefer.

directions: Plant bulbs about 3 inches deep in fall in gritty but humus-rich soil in sun to light shade. This rare species bears soft primrose-yellow flowers finely dotted with green, blooming very early. Unlike most dwarf irises it dislikes a hot dry summer, so give it a cooler, evenly moist spot.

planting.note: Plant in fall in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in fall in gritty, well-drained soil. This early selection opens pale blue flowers heavily speckled and blotched with deep blue and white around a yellow ridge. It flowers before the foliage extends and pairs well with snowdrops and early crocus.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in a sunny, freely draining spot.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in fall in gritty soil in full sun. This selection carries mid-blue flowers with a large white and orange patch on each fall. It flowers early and reliably and looks especially good massed in shallow bulb pans.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant corms about 3 inches deep and 2 to 3 inches apart in fall in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. The flowers are violet-blue on the outside of the petals and white within, opening wide in the early spring sun. Snow crocus bloom before the Dutch types and naturalize well in lawns and rock gardens.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in full sun with good drainage.

directions: Plant corms 3 inches deep and a couple of inches apart in fall in sharply drained soil. The deep golden-yellow flowers are marked with a bronze-purple base, glowing in the early spring light. Plant in generous groups where they can naturalize into cheerful early drifts.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in a sunny, freely draining bed.

directions: Plant corms 3 inches deep and 2 to 3 inches apart in fall in well-drained soil in full sun. The soft yellow flowers are feathered with bronze on the outer petals and open flat in the spring sun. It naturalizes readily in short grass and at the front of borders.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Plant corms about 3 inches deep and 2 to 3 inches apart in autumn in gritty, well-drained soil. The flowers open a soft silvery-blue with a paler throat and bright orange stigmas. Group them in sunny drifts where they will naturalize and bloom well before the larger Dutch crocus.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in a hot, sharply drained, sunny position.

directions: Plant corms 3 inches deep and a couple of inches apart in autumn in very free-draining soil in full sun. The deep-yellow flowers are heavily feathered and stained bronze-purple on the outside, with a glossy sheen. This early species relishes a hot, dry summer baking and suits a raised bed or rock garden.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in a warm, sunny, well-drained site.

directions: Plant corms about 3 inches deep in autumn in sharply drained soil in full sun. The buds show buff-fawn outer petals boldly feathered with purple, opening to reveal rich lilac interiors in the sun. It is one of the earliest crocus to bloom and prefers a warm, sheltered position.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in moisture-retentive soil in part shade.

directions: Plant bulbs about 3 inches deep in fall in cool, humus-rich soil in light shade. Each stem carries a loose spike of small, soft powder-blue star flowers in mid-spring. It naturalizes vigorously in woodland conditions and can self-sow freely where happy.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in a sunny or lightly shaded, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant bulbs about 3 inches deep in fall in well-drained soil in sun to light shade. The starry deep-blue flowers open in mid-spring on short, loose stems above strappy leaves. It spreads slowly into clumps and is a good, undemanding squill for the border front.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in late summer so they can flower in early autumn.

directions: Plant bulbs about 3 inches deep in late summer in gritty, sharply drained soil in full sun. Unlike its spring relatives, it sends up slender spikes of small lilac-pink to violet star flowers in autumn, often before the leaves. Give it a hot, dry position such as a rock garden or gravel bed where it can bake.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in cool, humus-rich soil in dappled shade.

directions: Plant the distinctive yellow, lily-like scaly bulbs about 3 to 4 inches deep in fall in leafy woodland soil. In mid-spring it raises open conical spikes of blue-violet stars above broad, glossy leaves. It thrives in cool, moist part shade and slowly forms substantial clumps.

planting.note: Set rhizomes out once soil has warmed and hard frost has passed.

directions: Plant rhizomes about 4 inches deep in rich, constantly moist soil, ideally at a pond edge or in a boggy bed. Keep the roots wet through the growing season and feed monthly for a long run of pure white spathes. In marginal zones mulch heavily or lift and store the rhizome frost-free over winter.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring after frost danger eases.

directions: Set bulbs 5 inches deep in fertile, free-draining soil where they get plenty of summer sun. The pale-green flowers are edged in maroon and crowned with a green leafy topknot on speckled stems. Keep evenly watered in growth, then dry the bulb off and protect from hard freezes over winter.

planting.note: Plant large bulbs in spring in a deep, rich bed.

directions: Plant the big bulb 6 inches deep in deep, fertile, moisture-retentive soil in full sun. It sends up towering stems, sometimes over five feet, ending in a long pale-green pineapple-topped spike. Feed and water generously through summer, then lift or heavily protect the bulb from frost.

planting.note: Plant bulbs with the neck exposed for autumn to early winter bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs shallowly in free-draining soil or a container with the neck above the surface. Slender stems carry loose heads of small pink flowers with distinctly crimped, wavy-edged petals late in the year. Keep it dry during summer rest and shelter from hard frost.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring with necks exposed for large autumn blooms.

directions: Plant this vigorous hybrid shallowly at the foot of a sunny wall with the bulb neck above soil. It produces tall stems topped with large, well-filled heads of bright rose-pink flowers in autumn. Leave clumps to settle for the best display and keep them dry through their summer dormancy.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs shallowly for autumn flowers.

directions: Plant the small bulbs just below the surface in gritty, well-drained soil or a shallow pot. This miniature species carries dainty heads of frilled pale-pink flowers on wiry stems in autumn. Grasslike leaves persist into winter, so water lightly then and keep dry in summer, protecting from frost.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring after frost for summer bloom.

directions: Set bulbs about 4 inches deep with the neck near the surface in well-drained, fertile soil. This hybrid opens elegant white flowers with a fringed central cup and reflexed, arching petals in early summer. Lift and dry the bulbs for frost-free winter storage where the ground freezes.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant bulbs about 4 inches deep with the neck at the surface in fertile, free-draining soil. This selection bears soft primrose-yellow flowers with a frilled cup and long slender petals in summer. Give it warmth and steady moisture in growth, then store the dried bulbs frost-free over winter.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring for mid- to late-summer bloom.

directions: Plant corms 3 to 4 inches deep in clumps in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. Arching sprays of large downward-facing orange flowers, each marked with a deep mahogany-red throat, open in late summer. Leave clumps to bulk up, dividing every few years, and mulch in colder zones.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring for summer flowers.

directions: Plant corms about 4 inches deep in groups in free-draining soil in a sunny spot. Slender flower spikes carry warm golden-yellow to amber blooms above pleated sword-shaped leaves in summer. Lift and divide congested clumps in spring, and protect the corms with mulch where winters are hard.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring for large late-summer flowers.

directions: Plant corms 3 to 4 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. This variety is prized for its very large, outward-facing clear-orange flowers with paler centers in late summer. Give it a warm site, leave clumps undisturbed to establish, and mulch the corms over winter in cold areas.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring in a warm, sheltered spot.

directions: Plant corms about 3 inches deep in free-draining soil in full sun to show off the bronze-tinted foliage. Soft apricot-yellow flowers open against the smoky bronze leaves in mid- to late summer. It appreciates a warm, sheltered position and reliable winter mulch, as it is a little less hardy than many montbretias.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring for summer bloom.

directions: Plant corms 3 to 4 inches deep in clumps in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. Branching stems bear glowing coppery-orange flowers with soft apricot centers over ribbed green leaves. Leave clumps to multiply, divide in spring when crowded, and mulch through winter in colder gardens.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring for bright summer color.

directions: Plant corms about 3 inches deep in groups in free-draining, fertile soil in full sun. Wiry arching stems carry vivid scarlet-orange star-shaped flowers above narrow sword leaves in summer. Leave undisturbed to form drifts, divide every few years, and protect corms with mulch in cold winters.

planting.note: Plant corms after frost and stagger plantings for a long season.

directions: Plant corms 4 to 6 inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun, spacing successive batches two weeks apart. This large-flowered variety sends up tall spikes packed with deep velvety-red blooms in summer. Stake in windy sites and lift the corms for dry, frost-free storage where winters freeze.

planting.note: Plant corms after frost, succession planting for continuous bloom.

directions: Set corms 4 to 6 inches deep in fertile, free-draining soil in full sun. Spikes open with soft yellow-green flowers marked by a bold wine-red blotch at the throat in midsummer. Stake tall stems, cut spikes for the vase as the lowest buds color, and lift the corms before hard frost.

planting.note: Plant corms after frost in warm soil for summer spikes.

directions: Plant corms 4 to 6 inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun, staggering plantings for succession. Tall, well-filled spikes carry violet-blue flowers with subtle paler markings in mid- to late summer. Support the stems in exposed spots and store lifted corms dry and frost-free over winter.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn for late-spring flowers in mild climates.

directions: Plant corms 3 to 4 inches deep in free-draining soil in full sun, ideally in autumn where winters are mild. Loose one-sided spikes of magenta-pink flowers appear in late spring above narrow ribbed leaves. This wild Mediterranean species is drought-tolerant once established and can naturalize in warm gardens.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn or early spring for early-summer bloom.

directions: Plant corms about 3 inches deep in moisture-retentive but well-drained soil in sun or light shade. This cold-hardy species carries slender spikes of deep purple-pink flowers in early summer in meadows and damp grassland. It does not need lifting in most temperate gardens and can be left to naturalize.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring in gritty, sharply drained soil.

directions: Plant corms 3 inches deep in very sharply drained, gritty soil in full sun, as this species clings to sheer cliff faces in the wild. Short spikes of intense scarlet-red flowers open in summer above narrow leaves. Give it excellent drainage and keep the corms drier in dormancy to prevent rot.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost once soil is warm.

directions: Plant rhizomes 4 to 6 inches deep in rich, moist soil in full sun after the ground warms. The bold bronze-red leaves are edged in deep purple and set off spikes of small bright-red flowers. Feed and water generously through summer, then lift and store the rhizomes frost-free over winter.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in wet soil or pond margins.

directions: Plant rhizomes 4 inches deep in constantly wet, rich soil or in a basket at a pond edge. This water canna carries soft coral-pink flowers above slender blue-green foliage all summer. It thrives standing in shallow water; lift the rhizome and keep it damp and frost-free over winter.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in wet soil or shallow water.

directions: Plant rhizomes 4 inches deep in saturated, fertile soil or submerge the container at a pond margin. This water canna produces clear yellow flowers over tall blue-green foliage through summer. Keep the roots permanently wet in growth and overwinter the rhizome damp and above freezing.

planting.note: Set the bulb out once the soil has warmed and all frost has passed.

directions: Plant the bulb with its neck and shoulders sitting just above the soil line in a fast-draining, humus-rich mix. Water sparingly until leaves appear, then keep evenly moist through the growing season. Green-and-red streaked flowers open in early summer on stout stems once the clump is established.

planting.note: Plant after the last spring frost when night temperatures stay mild.

directions: Position the bulb so the top third stays exposed above a gritty, well-drained potting mix. Give bright, filtered light and let the surface dry slightly between waterings. Creamy petals heavily spotted with crimson appear in late spring; feed monthly while foliage is active.

planting.note: This epiphytic species prefers to be started in warm, humid conditions after frost.

directions: Grow this tree-dwelling species in a loose, bark-based epiphyte mix rather than heavy soil, keeping the bulb barely covered. Provide warmth, humidity, and dappled shade, letting roots dry briefly between waterings. Unusual matte green flowers with a metallic sheen open in spring and last well.

planting.note: Set the bulb out in warm soil after frost danger has passed.

directions: Plant the bulb with its neck just clear of a rich, free-draining soil and water steadily as growth begins. This sterile triploid rarely sets seed but bulks up readily from offsets, so divide crowded clumps every few years. Salmon-red trumpet flowers rise in late summer to autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring once the ground has warmed thoroughly.

directions: Give this dry-country species a sharply drained, sandy soil and full sun, keeping the bulb tip above the surface. Water during active growth but allow a distinct dry rest after the leaves fade. Pale yellow to cream flowers appear in early summer; it is an important parent of many yellow garden hybrids.

planting.note: Set bulbs out after the last frost in warm, well-drained ground.

directions: Plant the large bulb with a third of it standing proud of a rich, gritty mix and provide bright filtered light. Keep barely moist until the flower stalk emerges, then water and feed regularly. Broad red-and-white flowers with a green throat open in spring; this Andean species underlies much of the large-flowered Dutch stock.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost, or grow year-round as a container bulb.

directions: Set the bulb with its shoulders above a rich, free-draining soil and stake the tall stems if grown in wind. Water freely in growth and reduce it as foliage yellows to encourage a rest period. Several white blooms boldly striped in red are carried on each stout scape in late spring.

planting.note: Pot up indoors for winter bloom or set outside after frost in warm zones.

directions: Nestle the bulb in a snug pot with the upper third exposed and water sparingly until the shoot lengthens. Keep it in bright light and turn the pot so the stem grows straight. Large, deep velvety-red trumpets open roughly six to ten weeks after potting; cure and store the bulb dry to rebloom.

planting.note: Force indoors in winter or plant outdoors after frost where hardy.

directions: Plant the bulb high in a well-drained mix, leaving the neck clear, and water lightly until growth is underway. Provide bright light and steady warmth for even stem development. Soft chartreuse-green flowers with a lighter throat open about six to ten weeks after potting.

planting.note: Best forced indoors in the cool season or grown as a container bulb.

directions: Set the bulb with its shoulders above a fast-draining mix and water sparingly until the bud shows. Keep it warm and brightly lit, feeding after flowering to rebuild the bulb. Large ivory-white flowers washed and veined with cool green open on tall scapes in six to ten weeks.

planting.note: Force in pots for holiday color or plant out after frost in mild climates.

directions: Pot the bulb firmly with the top third above the soil and water lightly until leaves and bud emerge. Give bright light and rotate the pot for a straight stem, staking the heavy heads if needed. Fully double flowers ruffled in red and creamy white open about six to ten weeks after planting.

planting.note: Force indoors in winter or set outside after the last frost where hardy.

directions: Plant the bulb high in a snug container and water sparingly until the shoot is well up. Keep warm and brightly lit, then support the substantial flower stems. Pure double white blooms with just a hint of green at the base open roughly six to ten weeks after potting.

planting.note: Grow as a forced pot bulb indoors or plant out after frost in warm zones.

directions: Set the bulb with its neck clear of a well-drained mix and water lightly until active growth begins. Provide bright, even light and stake the top-heavy stems as flowers form. Rich double cherry-red blooms open about six to ten weeks after potting and last well as cut stems.

planting.note: Force indoors in the cool season or plant out after frost where hardy.

directions: Pot the bulb high with a third exposed and water sparingly until the flower stalk appears. Keep it warm and brightly lit for compact, sturdy growth. Soft salmon-pink flowers with a lighter throat open on medium stems about six to ten weeks after planting.

planting.note: Plant or divide clumps in spring in a shaded, sheltered spot.

directions: Set divisions in a rich, freely draining woodland soil in dappled to full shade, keeping the fleshy roots intact. This species develops a distinct aerial stem over time, so give it room and shelter from strong sun. Pendent orange-and-green tubular flowers appear in spring; water regularly in growth and ease off in winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a moist, shaded position that stays frost-free.

directions: Plant this large swamp-forest species in a rich, moisture-retentive but draining soil in deep shade. Unlike its drier relatives it tolerates consistently damp ground, so water generously through the warm months. Tall stems carry drooping orange tubular flowers in late winter to spring, often with a second autumn flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a gritty, well-drained soil with afternoon shade.

directions: This drought-adapted species from a winter-rainfall region needs sharp drainage and more sun tolerance than other clivias. Water through the cooler months and keep it drier in summer, protecting it from hard frost. Nodding orange-and-yellow flowers with green tips open in spring above strap leaves marked by a pale central stripe.

planting.note: Plant or divide in spring in a shaded, frost-protected bed or container.

directions: Grow this yellow-flowered form in a rich, well-drained soil in bright to deep shade, keeping the crown at soil level. Water freely in the growing season and give a cool, drier winter rest to set buds. Clusters of clear soft-yellow trumpet flowers open in spring, followed by ornamental yellow berries.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade; this hybrid is best increased by division.

directions: This old garden hybrid between Clivia miniata and Clivia nobilis thrives in the same rich, shaded, well-drained conditions as its parents. Keep it evenly moist while growing and cooler and drier in winter. Dense heads of pendent orange tubular flowers appear over a long spring season, often reblooming intermittently.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a moist to wet, sunny position near ponds or streams.

directions: Plant this large Australian bulb with its neck at the surface in rich, constantly moist soil, including shallow water margins. It forms a bold clump with broad arching leaves and tolerates coastal salt spray. Rounded heads of white spidery flowers open in warm weather, followed by large fleshy seeds.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a pond margin or bed that stays wet in summer.

directions: This South African species grows in seasonal pans, so give it soil that floods in summer and dries in winter. Plant the bulb just below the surface and keep it wet through the growing season. Bell-shaped flowers in shades of pink to reddish appear on slender stems in summer above narrow keeled leaves.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for this winter-growing, summer-dormant species.

directions: Plant this winter-rainfall bulb with its neck exposed in gritty, well-drained soil and water from autumn through spring. Allow a warm, dry rest once the leaves die back in summer. Large flowers open white and age to deep pink, giving a two-toned look across the cluster in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in warm, well-drained ground once frost has passed.

directions: Set the large bulb with its neck at the surface in rich, free-draining soil and water well during the warm growing season. This tropical East African species resents cold, so grow it under glass in marginal climates. Fragrant white flowers boldly striped with a deep purplish-red keel open in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, sharply drained grassland-style bed.

directions: This compact grassland crinum wants a gritty, well-drained soil and full sun, with the bulb neck just showing. Water during summer growth and keep it drier when dormant. Pink-flushed white flowers striped along each petal appear in summer, sometimes prompted by the first rains after a dry spell.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in a gritty, well-drained soil in a warm sheltered spot.

directions: This rare second species of true Amaryllis needs very sharp drainage and a warm, dry summer dormancy. Plant the bulb with its neck exposed and water only from autumn through spring. A rounded head of many pink trumpet flowers rises on a bare stem in late summer to autumn, ahead of the broad two-ranked leaves.

planting.note: Plant in late summer to autumn with the bulb neck at the surface.

directions: Plant this white-flowered selection at the base of a warm wall in full sun with sharp drainage, leaving the bulb neck exposed. Leave clumps undisturbed to flower well and keep them dry during summer dormancy. Fragrant pure-white trumpets open on bare stems in late summer, with strap leaves following in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in late summer or autumn with the bulb tip near the surface.

directions: This cross between Amaryllis belladonna and Nerine bowdenii wants a warm, sharply drained spot in full sun. Plant with the neck barely covered and keep it dry through summer dormancy. Airy heads of glistening pink flowers rise on bare stems in autumn, blending the size of one parent with the sparkle of the other.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a very well-drained, sunny position or container.

directions: Grow this slow, long-lived species in gritty, sharply drained soil with the bulb neck exposed, ideally in a pot that can be kept dry when resting. Water sparingly and avoid disturbing the roots. Nodding tubular flowers shading from red at the base through yellow to green tips open on a stout stem, mainly in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a well-drained, part-shaded spot or pot.

directions: Set the small bulb just below the surface in a humus-rich, freely draining mix and water during the growing season. Give it bright light with some shade from the hottest sun and a drier winter rest. One or two exceptionally large, glowing scarlet trumpet flowers open per stem in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, well-drained soil with the bulb high.

directions: Plant this cliff-dwelling species with much of the large bulb above a sharply drained mix, as it naturally perches on rock ledges. Water in growth and keep nearly dry when dormant. Pendent tubular flowers in soft coral to salmon hang from an arching stem in spring, above distinctly sickle-shaped grey-green leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, well-drained grassland-style bed.

directions: Grow this summer-growing grassland bulb in a well-drained soil in full sun, planting with the neck near the surface. Water through the warm months and rest it drier in winter. A cluster of curved red tubular flowers opens on an erect stem in late spring to summer, sometimes flushed by fire or heavy rain in the wild.

planting.note: Set out young plants a few weeks before the last frost since they tolerate light chill.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed indoors and press lightly without covering, as light aids germination. Grow on cool and pinch once to encourage the strong central spikes this tall series is known for. Cut stems for the vase when the lowest third of florets have opened.

planting.note: Transplant hardened seedlings around the last frost for early cool-season blooms.

directions: Start seed indoors on the soil surface under bright light and keep the mix evenly moist until sprouts appear. This tall Group 4 type responds to cool nights and long days by throwing long, sturdy stems ideal for market bunches. Harvest when a few basal florets show color to maximize vase life.

planting.note: Move plants outside as the soil warms while nights are still cool.

directions: Press seed onto a fine sterile mix and leave uncovered under gentle warmth and light. Support the intermediate-height spikes with netting so the flower stems stay straight for cutting. Snip when the bottom florets open and strip lower leaves before setting stems in cool water.

planting.note: Plant out a couple weeks before the last frost for early bedding color.

directions: Sow indoors on the surface and keep bright and evenly moist for even emergence. This compact, open-faced type blooms early and needs no pinching for a mounded display. Deadhead spent spikes to keep new flushes of the bell-shaped florets coming.

planting.note: Set out hardened seedlings shortly before the last expected frost.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed and provide bright light without burying it. Prized for orange florets flamed with gold, this mid-height variety benefits from a single pinch to branch well. Cut spikes as the lowest blooms open for warm-toned bouquets.

planting.note: Transplant seedlings out as frosts ease and the ground begins to warm.

directions: Press seed lightly onto moist medium and keep uncovered under good light. This heirloom carries flecked and striped florets in many colors, so each spike differs from the next. Harvest for cutting when the bottom florets open and enjoy the candy-stripe patterning in the vase.

planting.note: Plant out a couple of weeks ahead of the last frost for early borders.

directions: Sow on the surface indoors and keep the seedbed lit and lightly moist. This bushy dwarf with open azalea-type florets makes a tidy edging and needs no staking. Shear lightly after the first flush to prompt fresh bloom into cooler weather.

planting.note: Stock craves cool weather, so set plants out several weeks before the last frost.

directions: Sow indoors and grow the seedlings cool, culling weak singles once true leaves show if selecting for doubles. This tall single-stem series times its bloom under cool nights and produces fragrant column spikes for cutting. Harvest when about half the florets are open for the best vase life.

planting.note: Transplant in cool spring conditions well before heat arrives.

directions: Start seed indoors on the surface and keep temperatures cool for sturdy, disease-free seedlings. Grow in fertile, well-drained soil and provide steady moisture to fuel the tall fragrant spikes. Cut when the lower florets open and expect the sweet clove scent to fill a room.

planting.note: Set out early while nights stay cool for a quick, uniform crop.

directions: Sow indoors and grow on cool; this early series flowers quickly and holds a high percentage of doubles. Keep plants moist and avoid heat, which halts flowering and shortens the spikes. Cut single-stem plants once the basal florets open for market bunches.

planting.note: Transplant into warm soil after all danger of frost has passed.

directions: Surface-sow indoors on warm medium and press in lightly for good contact. This vigorous spiked type sends up tapered pink flower wands that pale to lavender-white and draw pollinators all season. Cut long stems for fresh or dried arrangements once the spikes color up.

planting.note: Plant out once nights stay warm to keep growth from stalling.

directions: Start seed warm indoors and grow the seedlings without any check to encourage tall, single stems. Space closely and support with netting so this cut-flower series produces the long, dense plumes florists want. Harvest when plumes are fully formed but before seed sets for the cleanest color.

planting.note: Set out in warm soil after frost for tall summer stems.

directions: Sow warm indoors and grow on strongly for the greatest stem length. This chartreuse-green spiked celosia gives soft, feathery wands prized as a filler in mixed bouquets. Cut when the spikes are firm and use fresh or hang to dry for lasting texture.

planting.note: Transplant only when the soil has warmed thoroughly after frost.

directions: Start seed warm and avoid any root disturbance, since checked crested types can flower prematurely on stunted plants. This heirloom cockscomb forms large velvety red combs on stout stems. Cut when the comb is fully developed and hang upside down in shade to dry.

planting.note: Wait for settled warm weather before planting out this bedding celosia.

directions: Sow indoors on warm medium and grow steadily to avoid premature cresting. This branching award-winner covers itself in many bright scarlet combs above dark-tinged foliage. Deadhead to prolong the show or leave combs to dry on the plant for cutting.

planting.note: Move plants out when soil is warm and frost risk has ended.

directions: Start seed warm indoors and grow the seedlings on without stress. This tall spiked variety produces upright rose-pink candles on long stems well suited to cutting. Harvest before pollen drops for clean color and long vase life.

planting.note: Transplant into warm beds or pots once frost has passed.

directions: Sow warm and keep young plants growing steadily to hold their compact habit. This well-branched series offers plumed and spiked forms that shrug off heat and bloom nonstop. Trim spent plumes for tidiness, though it flowers well without deadheading.

planting.note: Transplant to warm soil once frost danger is gone.

directions: Sow warm indoors and grow the seedlings on the dry side to avoid damping off. This species bears bright red-orange globes on strong stems and tolerates heat and drought with ease. Harvest heads for fresh arranging or dry them for their long-lasting scarlet color.

planting.note: Plant out only after the soil warms well past frost.

directions: Start warm indoors and grow seedlings evenly for the tall, uniform stems this cut series is bred for. The color-separate globes in carmine, purple, orange, and white hold well in the field and vase. Cut when heads are firm and strip lower leaves before bunching.

planting.note: Move plants outside after frost when the ground is warm.

directions: Sow warm and grow on without overwatering to keep the compact habit tight. This dwarf covers itself in deep purple globes and makes an even edging or container filler. It needs no deadheading and shrugs off heat, drought, and humidity.

planting.note: Transplant into warm beds once frost has passed.

directions: Start seed warm and keep plants on the dry side for compact, floriferous growth. This mid-height series offers pink, purple, and white globes on tidy mounds that bloom nonstop in heat. Cut for small posies or leave the plants to color beds all summer.

planting.note: Plant out to warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow warm indoors and grow on evenly for well-branched plants carrying large round heads. This series produces oversized globes in strong colors on stems long enough for cutting. Harvest when heads firm up, and enjoy their lasting color fresh or dried.

planting.note: Plant around the last frost once the soil is warm.

directions: Surface-sow and barely cover, keeping the flat warm and moist. This chartreuse form trails long lime-green ropes that make a fresh, unusual filler in bouquets. Harvest the tassels when firm and use them fresh, as the green fades somewhat when dried.

planting.note: Sow or transplant around the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Barely cover the seed on warm mix and keep it moist until germination. This heirloom is named for the large, drooping purple-maroon flower head that curves like an elephant's trunk. Cut the striking heads for fresh use or hang them to dry for dramatic everlasting displays.

planting.note: Set out around the last frost for a long cutting season.

directions: Start seed indoors and grow the seedlings on in bright light for strong, uniform plants. Bred for cutting, this color-separate series yields long stems with dense papery sprays. Harvest when nearly all florets are open and dry in bunches for reliable everlastings.

planting.note: Plant near the last frost into well-drained soil.

directions: Sow indoors and keep patient, as germination is gradual over two to three weeks. This series offers a full range of clear, bright colors on well-branched cutting stems. Cut sprays when the florets have opened and hang to dry for long-lasting arrangements.

planting.note: Set out after frost; treat as an annual outside mild zones.

directions: Sow indoors and be patient with slow germination, then grow on in bright light. This species forms leathery basal rosettes topped by airy sprays of vivid purple-blue calyces. Cut the branching stems when well colored for fresh bouquets or drying, and give it lean, fast-draining soil.

planting.note: Transplant near the last frost into warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Press seed onto the surface and keep it lit and lightly moist until sprouting. This series produces extra-large double blooms on tall, sturdy stems ideal for cutting. Harvest when flowers are half open and hang to dry for big, colorful everlastings.

planting.note: Plant out around the last frost when soil warms.

directions: Surface-sow and leave uncovered under bright light for germination. This dwarf mix covers compact plants in papery blooms of red, rose, yellow, orange, and white for beds and pots. Deadhead for continued bloom or cut a few stems to dry.

planting.note: Set out near the last frost into warm ground.

directions: Sow on the surface and keep lit and lightly moist until seedlings appear. This tall strain bears large, fully double flowers in a wide color range on long cutting stems. Harvest half-open blooms and hang them to dry for premium everlasting bunches.

planting.note: Set out after frost, giving this tall selection room at the back of a bed.

directions: This large heirloom form is grown from cuttings and reaches shrub size in a single season, so give it plenty of space. Provide rich soil, regular water, and support in windy sites. Deep wine-red flowers held in dusky calyces open from late summer through autumn and are a magnet for hummingbirds.

planting.note: Plant out after frost; it may overwinter where roots do not freeze hard.

directions: Grown from cuttings, this vigorous selection quickly forms a tall clump, so give it a foot and a half to two and a half feet of space. Provide fertile soil and steady moisture in full sun to light shade. Deep purple flowers set in near-black calyces bloom from early summer to frost and are irresistible to hummingbirds.

planting.note: Set out after the last frost into a warm, sheltered spot.

directions: This sterile hybrid is propagated only from cuttings and grows into a rounded, free-flowering mound. Plant in rich, well-drained soil and water regularly, feeding lightly for best performance. Magenta-pink tubular flowers with plum calyces bloom from late spring until frost and rarely need deadheading.

planting.note: Plant after frost where it can bask in full sun.

directions: Set out cutting-grown plants of this Wish-series hybrid after the danger of frost, spacing them for good air movement. Keep the soil moist but never soggy and shear lightly if growth flops. Rich burgundy-purple flowers emerge from deep maroon calyces and continue from early summer into autumn.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into full sun and warm soil.

directions: Plant cutting-grown starts after frost in a sunny bed with fertile, draining soil. Water steadily and trim spent stems to keep the plant tidy and blooming. Coral-orange tubular flowers in dark calyces cover the plant from late spring to frost, drawing hummingbirds all season.

planting.note: Set out after frost; it blooms late, so start plants early.

directions: This statuesque Brazilian species is usually grown from cuttings and needs a long, warm season to flower, so plant it early and large. Give it fertile soil, ample room, and shelter from strong wind. Long, arching spikes densely packed with fuzzy orange-red flowers on rust-colored stems appear in late summer and autumn.

planting.note: Set out after frost; the showy late bloom needs a long season.

directions: This tall selection is grown from cuttings and needs plenty of room to reach its shrubby full size. Plant in rich soil with steady moisture and stake if the site is exposed. Deep cobalt-blue flowers cradled in striking chartreuse calyces open in autumn, making a bold late-season display for hummingbirds.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, well-drained sunny position.

directions: This aromatic Mexican shrub is easily grown from cuttings and forms a rounded, twiggy mound. Give it full sun and free-draining soil, watering only during extended dry spells once established. Small red to magenta flowers with blackcurrant-scented leaves bloom from late spring until frost; cut back hard in spring to renew it.

planting.note: Sow around the last frost date directly where plants are to grow.

directions: Sow seed directly in the garden around the last frost, thinning seedlings to stand about a hand's width apart. The showy part is not the tiny true flowers but the brilliant pink, purple, and white bracts atop each stem. Cut stems just as the bracts color for fresh or dried arrangements, and it will often self-sow.

planting.note: Plant out after frost into rich soil with afternoon shade in hot areas.

directions: Grow this glossy-leaved Mexican species from cuttings and plant it where it gets morning sun and some afternoon relief. Keep the soil consistently moist and feed lightly for continuous flowering. Vivid magenta-pink blooms appear over a long season and it makes an excellent container or hanging-basket plant.

planting.note: Plant after frost into full sun for best flowering.

directions: This vigorous, cutting-grown selection is set out after frost and needs no deadheading to stay covered in bloom. Give it full sun and moderate water in beds or containers where heat is no obstacle. Hundreds of orange, cigar-shaped flowers appear from late spring to frost, making it a hummingbird favorite.

planting.note: Set out after frost into warm, sunny beds or pots.

directions: Start the fine seed indoors pressed onto the surface and move seedlings out after frost into warm soil. This heat-lover needs full sun and moderate water and rarely requires deadheading. Slender spikes of snapdragon-like flowers in purple, blue, pink, or white bloom from early summer straight through to frost.

planting.note: Plant after frost into full sun and warm soil.

directions: This vigorous cutting-grown selection grows taller and fuller than seed types and needs no deadheading. Set out after frost into full sun with average, well-drained soil and steady moisture. Upright spikes of deep blue-violet flowers rise above the foliage all summer, excellent for cutting and containers.

planting.note: Plant after frost; this selection is unusually cold-hardy.

directions: This exceptionally hardy cutting-grown selection can overwinter in the ground where many lantanas cannot, returning as a large shrub. Plant after frost in full sun with excellent drainage and mulch the crown in colder zones. Clusters of orange, coral, and yellow flowers bloom prolifically all summer and feed butterflies.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun; nearly sterile so it sets few berries.

directions: This low, spreading cutting-grown selection is nearly seedless, so it pours its energy into flowers instead of berries. Plant after frost in full sun and lean soil, watering sparingly once established. Golden-yellow flower clusters blanket the mounding foliage from late spring to frost and are magnets for butterflies.

planting.note: Sow as soon as the soil can be worked in early spring, or in autumn in mild-winter areas.

directions: Nick or soak the hard seed overnight before sowing about one inch deep at the base of tall supports. This Spencer-type carries large, frilled crimson-scarlet blooms on long stems ideal for cutting. Keep the roots cool and evenly moist, and pick flowers often to keep new buds forming.

planting.note: Sow in early spring once soil is workable, or overwinter autumn-sown plants in a cold frame.

directions: Start seed one inch deep, either direct-sown or in deep pots for transplanting after the worst frosts pass. The soft cream, well-scented flowers hold well in a vase and grow best on rich, deeply dug soil. Train the shoots up netting or canes and deadhead regularly to extend the season.

planting.note: Sow in early spring as the ground thaws, or in autumn under cover for the earliest flowers.

directions: Soak seed overnight and sow one inch deep at the foot of tall canes or a trellis. Rich, deep, mid-blue blooms are strongly scented and carried on long stems for cutting. Feed while in bud and pick flowers every few days to keep the plant productive.

planting.note: Sow when soil is workable in spring, or in deep pots in autumn for overwintering.

directions: Sow the pre-soaked seed about one inch deep next to sturdy vertical supports. Salmon-cream flowers with a lightly ruffled form appear on long, straight stems for cutting. Water steadily during dry spells and remove faded blooms to prevent seed set.

planting.note: An early heirloom that flowers quickly; sow in early spring or autumn in mild regions.

directions: Sow seed one inch deep in rich soil at the base of a trellis or fence. This old bicolor bears rose-pink standards over pale creamy-white wings with a good perfume. It is early and free-flowering, so pick often and keep well watered to prolong bloom.

planting.note: Sow in early spring once soil warms, or autumn-sow under glass in mild climates.

directions: Nick the seed coat, soak overnight, and sow one inch deep beside tall supports. This Victorian grandiflora offers soft lavender-blue flowers with an outstanding fragrance. Grow in cool, moist, fertile soil and cut stems regularly for the vase.

planting.note: Sow in early spring or autumn; keep young plants cool and pinched for bushiness.

directions: Sow the soaked seed one inch deep at the base of netting or canes. Deep mid-blue, waved flowers carry a rich scent on long cutting stems. Pinch seedlings when a few inches tall to encourage side shoots, and deadhead to keep bloom coming.

planting.note: Sow when soil is workable in spring, or start in autumn under cover for early crops.

directions: Soak seed overnight, then sow one inch deep in well-prepared soil beside supports. Large, pure white waved flowers on long stems make an elegant cut flower. Provide steady moisture and a high-potash feed once buds appear, cutting frequently to keep plants blooming.

planting.note: Sow in early spring or autumn; grows tall so provide sturdy supports.

directions: Sow pre-soaked seed one inch deep at the foot of tall canes. Rich rosy-mauve, ruffled blooms are strongly perfumed and excellent for cutting. Keep roots cool and well watered, feed while budding, and pick often to prolong flowering.

planting.note: Sow in early spring once frosts ease, or in autumn under glass in mild areas.

directions: Sow seed one inch deep in fertile, deeply worked soil beside a trellis. Bright lilac-blue, waved flowers are well scented and carried on long, strong stems. Water consistently, feed when in bud, and cut blooms regularly to keep the plant flowering.

planting.note: Sow in early spring or overwinter autumn-sown plants in a cold frame.

directions: Soak seed overnight and sow one inch deep at the base of canes or netting. The white ground is delicately flushed and streaked with soft blue-mauve, and the flowers are richly scented. Grow cool and moist, and pick stems frequently for the vase.

planting.note: Sow in early spring when soil is workable, or in autumn under cover.

directions: Sow the pre-soaked seed one inch deep beside tall vertical supports. Deep maroon-crimson, velvety flowers carry a strong scent on long cutting stems. Keep soil moist and fertile, feed while in bud, and deadhead to extend the flowering season.

planting.note: Sow in early spring or autumn; keep young plants cool for strong stems.

directions: Sow seed one inch deep at the foot of canes or a trellis in rich soil. Unusual pale blue flowers with a soft turquoise cast are well scented and good for cutting. Provide even moisture, pinch seedlings once, and pick often to keep buds forming.

planting.note: Sow in early spring or autumn; an old grandiflora with strong scent.

directions: Soak seed overnight and sow one inch deep in fertile soil beside supports. This heritage variety bears creamy primrose-ivory flowers with an intense old-fashioned fragrance. Grow in cool, moist conditions and cut the fragrant stems regularly for indoors.

planting.note: Sow in early spring when workable, or autumn-sow in mild-winter gardens.

directions: Sow the soaked seed one inch deep at the base of tall canes or netting. This vintage grandiflora produces bright scarlet-red flowers with a good scent. Keep well watered in warm weather, feed while in bud, and deadhead to prolong the display.

planting.note: Sow in early spring or autumn under cover for the earliest flowers.

directions: Sow seed one inch deep in deeply dug, fertile soil beside supports. Large pure white, well-waved flowers on long stems are excellent for exhibition and cutting. Water steadily, feed when in bud, and pick frequently to keep new blooms coming.

planting.note: Sow in early spring or autumn; a pale pastel that shows best in cool weather.

directions: Soak seed and sow one inch deep at the foot of canes or a trellis. Soft pale blue, waved flowers with a gentle scent are carried on long cutting stems. Keep the roots cool and moist and pick regularly to maintain a long flowering run.

planting.note: Sow in early spring or autumn; provide tall, sturdy supports.

directions: Sow pre-soaked seed one inch deep in rich soil beside canes or netting. Deep navy to violet-blue, waved flowers are strongly scented and long-stemmed for cutting. Keep evenly moist, feed when budding, and deadhead to extend the season.

planting.note: Sow in early spring; a compact spreader for edging, pots and baskets.

directions: Sow seed one inch deep in containers, baskets or along a border edge. This low, trailing dwarf needs no support and bears bicolored pink-and-white scented flowers. Keep the compost moist, feed lightly through summer, and pick or trim spent blooms to keep it flowering.

planting.note: Sow in early spring; a mounding dwarf ideal for pots and edging.

directions: Sow seed one inch deep in containers or at the front of a sunny bed. This low, spreading dwarf carries pure white, lightly scented flowers without needing support. Keep evenly watered, feed through the season, and remove faded flowers to prolong bloom.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost danger passes and the soil warms.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep where the plants will flower, thinning to give each room. This tidy bush type shows its cherry-rose, upward-facing blooms clear of the leaves. Keep soil lean and only moderately watered, and harvest the peppery flowers and foliage for salads.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Sow the seeds half an inch deep directly in the border or a container. This compact strain carries soft creamy-yellow, upward-facing flowers held above neat foliage. Grow in poor to average soil with modest water, and pick the flowers and young leaves for the kitchen.

planting.note: Direct sow once frost has passed and the ground has warmed.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep where the plants are to grow and thin as needed. The pale peach-cream flowers carry a soft red blotch at the center and sit above compact foliage. Keep the soil lean and lightly watered for the best flowering, and use the edible blooms as a garnish.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost once the soil has warmed; a classic compact dwarf.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep directly in the garden or in pots and window boxes. This old dwarf bush strain forms neat mounds topped with flowers in a mix of yellow, orange, red and mahogany. Grow in lean soil with little feeding, and pick the edible flowers and leaves for salads.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; semi-trailing plants good for banks and containers.

directions: Sow the large seeds half an inch deep where the stems can trail freely. This semi-double Gleam blend flowers in a mix of yellow, orange, salmon and scarlet with a soft scent. Grow in poor to average soil with sparing water, and cascade the plants from pots or use as a spreading cover.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost at the base of a fence, trellis or bank to climb or trail.

directions: Sow the seeds half an inch deep near a support or where they can scramble over ground. These vigorous, long-stemmed plants climb by coiling leaf stalks and flower in a mix of warm colors. Grow in poor to average soil with modest water, and guide the shoots onto netting or let them trail from a wall.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; a very compact dwarf for edges and pots.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep in containers or at the front of a bed. This dwarf, mounding type carries clear orange flowers above compact green foliage. Keep the soil lean and only lightly watered, and pick the edible flowers and leaves as they open.
